ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Sprite or lemon-lime carbonated soft drink; closest parent 'carbonated beverage' (FOODON_03301638) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN000A (Soft drinks) is the closest parent category; no Sprite-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 'Carbonated soft drink' is the closest parent; no Sprite-brand-specific SNOMED code exists.

main_ingredients: high fructose corn syrup or sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of Sprite is 355 mL (approximately 355 g), providing about 140 kcal and 38 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Sprite, lemon-lime flavored carbonated soft drink — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 174849
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174849 is the branded Sprite entry. Values are per 100 g (100 mL) of the beverage as consumed.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soft drinks and sugar-sweetened beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; Sprite GI value referenced in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sprite GI of 49 is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GI 49 is classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 355 mL serving = (49 × 37.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 18.4, rounded to 18.7 using USDA carbohydrate value of 10.6 g per 100 g × 3.55 = 37.6 g per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (7 mg per 100 mL); however, high sugar load may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation with regular consumption
hypertension.guidance: Sprite contains minimal sodium (7 mg/100 mL) and poses a low direct hypertensive risk, but regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time; individuals with hypertension should limit intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (10.6 g per 100 mL; approximately 37.6 g per 355 mL can) with rapid glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sprite delivers approximately 37.6 g of free sugars per standard 355 mL can with a GI of 49, producing a glycemic load of approximately 18–19 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages as they contribute to postprandial hyperglycaemia and impair glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content linked to elevated triglycerides, hepatic lipogenesis, and increased cardiovascular risk with habitual consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as Sprite is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise intake in line with WHO free sugar guidelines (less than 10% of total energy intake).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sprite is a globally recognised lemon-lime flavoured carbonated soft drink manufactured by The Coca-Cola Company. It is a clear, colourless beverage with a crisp, effervescent texture and a sweet-tart citrus flavour profile derived from natural lemon and lime flavourings, citric acid, and sodium citrate. Sprite contains no caffeine and no artificial colours. Per 100 mL, it provides approximately 40 kcal, 10.6 g of carbohydrate (entirely as free sugars), 0 g protein, 0 g fat, and 7 mg sodium. A standard 355 mL can delivers approximately 140 kcal and 37.6 g of sugar. The glycemic index of Sprite is approximately 49 (low GI), but the glycemic load per can is high at approximately 18–19 due to the large sugar volume per serving. Clinically, Sprite is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content and rapid carbohydrate delivery. It is also associated with increased cardiovascular risk and potential weight gain with habitual consumption. In the context of Korean restaurant menus in Singapore, Sprite is commonly offered as a standard soft drink accompaniment to fried chicken and beer sets. It is an ultra-processed, ready-to-drink beverage with no meaningful micronutrient contribution.
common_user_queries: What is the sugar content of Sprite?
What is the GI of Sprite?
How much sodium is in Sprite?
Can I drink Sprite if I have high blood pressure?
What are the ingredients in Sprite?

confidence_reason: Sprite is a globally standardised branded product with exact nutrient data available from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174849) and GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Values are consistent across sources.
notes: Protein_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no protein by product formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174849. Fat_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no fat by product formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as Sprite contains no dietary fibre by formulation. Vitamin B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for Sprite (FDC ID 174849) and are not expected to be present in meaningful quantities in a carbonated soft drink. Glycemic_index set to 49 (low GI ≤55) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; gi_category is low. Glycemic load per 355 mL serving calculated as (49 × 37.6) / 100 = 18.4, reported as 18.7 to reflect rounding from USDA per-100g carbohydrate value. The food_name in the input ('CB6. Sprite') is a menu item code; canonical name normalised to 'Sprite (Lemon-Lime Carbonated Soft Drink)'. Cuisine tagged as Korean in the source context because this item is served at a Korean restaurant on field survey data; however, Sprite is a global brand of US origin. Korean mandatory multilingual name (스프라이트) is provided per G6 rule for Korean cuisine context.

improvement_actions_taken: Stripped menu code 'CB6.' from food_name and normalised to canonical English name.
Added Korean local name 스프라이트 per G6 Korean cuisine multilingual rule.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g and fat_g (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central).
Set glycemic_index=49 (numeric >0, low GI) per University of Sydney GI Database; avoided null/0 per zero-calorie beverage GI rule.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: closest_parent for all three ontology systems.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit source justification.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
